lessons.title: if consteval and auto Function Parameters

background.title: consteval in C++20
background.body: consteval (C++20) declares a function as "immediate": it can only be called in a constant evaluation context. Unlike constexpr, which can run both at compile time and at runtime, consteval always executes exclusively at compile time.

lessons.motivation: A constexpr function can be called both at compile time and at runtime. This flexibility is useful, but it creates a problem: if you want to call a consteval function (which is compile-time only) from inside a constexpr function, the compiler rejects it — it cannot know whether execution is happening at compile time. Before C++23, the only workaround was std::is_constant_evaluated() from C++20, but it had subtle pitfalls. Separately, auto in ordinary function parameters was only valid in lambdas. C++23 solves both issues: if consteval provides a reliable way to branch on the evaluation context, and auto in ordinary function parameters turns them into abbreviated function templates.
lessons.theory:
if consteval is a new branching construct introduced in C++23. Unlike if constexpr, it takes no condition expression: instead, it checks whether the current evaluation is happening in a constant evaluation context. The true branch executes only at compile time; the false branch executes only at runtime. The critical property: the true branch is itself a "manifestly constant-evaluated context", so consteval functions may be called inside it — exactly what was impossible with std::is_constant_evaluated(). A negated form is also available: if !consteval { ... } executes only at runtime.

The second C++23 feature is auto in ordinary function parameters. Since C++14, lambdas have supported auto parameters to create generic lambdas. C++23 extends this syntax to regular (named) functions. Writing void print(auto x) is equivalent to template<typename T> void print(T x): each auto parameter becomes a separate template parameter. This is called an "abbreviated function template".

auto parameters fully support qualifiers: const auto&, auto&&, auto*, and so on. Multiple auto parameters each introduce an independent template parameter — so void combine(auto a, auto b) can accept an int and a double simultaneously. During overload resolution, a function with a concrete type takes priority over a generic one: if both void describe(const std::string&) and void describe(const auto&) exist, the former is chosen for a std::string argument.

Both features make C++23 code more expressive without sacrificing performance. if consteval eliminates an entire class of errors that arose when consteval functions were called in the wrong context. auto parameters reduce template boilerplate: there is no need to write template<typename T> for straightforward generic functions.

examples.title: if consteval: different paths at compile time vs runtime
examples.explanation: my_sqrt is declared constexpr, so it can be called both at compile time and at runtime. Inside the if consteval branch the call to compile_time_sqrt is valid — we are guaranteed to be in a constant evaluation context. Without if consteval the compiler would reject the consteval call because it cannot know the context. Both paths produce the same result but use different implementations.

examples.title: auto function parameters: generic functions and overloading
examples.expectedOutput:
value: 42
value: 3.14
string of length 5: hello
add: 3
add: 4
add: foobar
examples.explanation: describe(const auto& x) is an abbreviated function template, equivalent to template<typename T> void describe(const T& x). When called with std::string{"hello"}, the compiler selects the more specialized overload describe(const std::string&). add(auto a, auto b) introduces two independent template parameters: a and b may have different types. For strings, operator+ performs concatenation.

lessons.title: Multidimensional operator[]: m[i, j] Syntax
lessons.motivation: Before C++23, operator[] could only take a single argument. Writing m[i, j] inside the brackets was interpreted by the compiler as the comma operator: m[i, j] meant m[(i, j)], which evaluates to m[j] — the right operand. C++20 deprecated this behaviour, and C++23 made it ill-formed. To index two-dimensional structures, developers resorted to workarounds: operator() in the style of m(i, j), chained subscripts m[i][j] via row proxy objects, or a helper index type m[{i, j}]. None of these feel natural for indexing. C++23 removed the restriction: operator[] can now accept any number of parameters, and commas inside [] separate arguments rather than invoking the comma operator.
lessons.theory:
C++23 allows operator[] to accept an arbitrary number of parameters — zero, one, two, or more. The declaration syntax is identical to any other operator overload: parameters are listed separated by commas. Commas inside [] at the call site now separate arguments rather than invoking the comma operator. The old behaviour (comma operator in subscript context) was deprecated in C++20 and became ill-formed in C++23, so legacy code that accidentally wrote m[i, j] to mean m[j] will no longer compile.

Parameters of operator[] can have any types and qualifiers. Both a mutating and a non-mutating overload can coexist: double& operator[](size_t i, size_t j) for writes and const double& operator[](size_t i, size_t j) const for reads. A variadic form using auto... is also valid, but for concrete data structures a fixed number of parameters with the appropriate types is more common.

The primary motivation for this language change was std::mdspan — a multidimensional array view added to the standard library in C++23. mdspan stores a pointer to a one-dimensional buffer and a mapping from multi-index to flat offset. The expression md[i, j] invokes a two-argument operator[] that computes the offset internally. Before this language change it was impossible to give mdspan an intuitive subscript interface, which is why the language feature and the library component landed together in C++23.

An operator with zero arguments — operator[]() — is technically valid since C++23, though rarely needed: it can be useful in proxy objects or DSL builders where a subscript without an index carries semantic meaning. One important note: the change only affects the subscript operator itself. Calling a[i, j] where a is a plain built-in array or a type that does not define a matching operator[] remains an error.

examples.title: Simple 3x3 matrix with operator[](size_t, size_t)
examples.explanation: Both overloads of operator[] take two size_t parameters. The expression m[0, 0] = 1.0 calls the non-const overload (returning double&), and m[i, j] in the loop body calls the const overload. Before C++23 this would not compile: the compiler would treat m[0, 0] as the comma operator and invoke a single-parameter operator[] with argument 0.

examples.title: 3D tensor with operator[](size_t, size_t, size_t)
examples.explanation: operator[] can now take three arguments. The syntax t[0, 0, 0] reads as naturally as indexing a built-in three-dimensional array. Internally the operator simply delegates to data[i][j][k] — no proxy objects required.

examples.title: Dynamic grid with bounds checking — the same pattern as std::mdspan
examples.expectedOutput:
g[0,0] = 1
g[1,2] = 5
g[2,3] = 9
g[0,1] = 0
caught: Grid: index out of range
examples.explanation: Grid stores its data in a flat std::vector and computes the offset i * cols + j inside operator[] — exactly what std::mdspan does under the hood. The non-const overload returns double& for writes; the const overload returns by value for reads. The bounds check shows that a two-parameter operator[] is not restricted in any way: it can contain arbitrary logic.

lessons.title: auto(x) / auto{x} and Lambda Improvements in C++23
lessons.motivation: In generic code you often need to make an explicit copy of a value whose type is not known in advance. Before C++23, the idiom for this was std::decay_t<decltype(x)>(x) — verbose, easy to get wrong, and hard to read at a glance. Separately, lambdas required an empty parameter list () even when there were no parameters but a specifier such as mutable, noexcept, or a trailing return type was present. C++23 addresses both problems: auto(x) / auto{x} is a new language construct for explicit decay-copy, and P1102 allows the () to be omitted from lambdas that carry specifiers but take no parameters.
lessons.theory:
The expression auto(x) produces a copy of x with its decayed type: references and cv-qualifiers are stripped, arrays decay to pointers, and function types decay to function pointers. This is exactly what happens when a value is passed to a template function by value, which is why the operation is called decay-copy. The brace form auto{x} is equivalent; the only difference is that it uses list-initialization. The primary use cases are lambda captures — [copy = auto(ref)] stores an owned copy instead of a dangling reference — and passing values to algorithms that may move from them. Before C++23 the only alternative was std::decay_t<decltype(x)>(x): equally correct, but substantially more verbose and harder to read.

The second C++23 change concerns lambdas: before C++23, an empty parameter list () was mandatory whenever a specifier appeared after the capture list — mutable, constexpr, noexcept, [[nodiscard]], or a trailing return type ->. C++23 (P1102) lifted this restriction: all of those specifiers are now valid immediately after [...] with no (). The result is cleaner syntax: auto f = [] mutable { ... } instead of auto f = []() mutable { ... }. Note that a plain []{ } with no parameters and no specifiers was already legal since C++11; the C++23 novelty is specifically the combination of "no parameters + specifiers present".

Also worth clarifying is the difference between [this] and [*this] in lambda captures. [this] captures the this pointer — the lambda accesses class members through that pointer, and if the object is destroyed before the lambda is called, the result is undefined behaviour. [*this] (C++17) captures a copy of the entire object, so the lambda holds its own instance and is independent of the original object's lifetime. C++23 introduced a third option — deducing this — which lets a lambda explicitly name a self parameter and deduce the object type at the call site; that technique is covered in detail in the next lesson.

All three topics share a common theme: C++23 removes boilerplate that was purely syntactic. auto(x) replaces a long decay_t trick, dropped () on lambdas eliminate noise, and [*this] capture has long provided a safe alternative to [this] without extra wrappers.

examples.title: auto(x) and auto{x}: decay-copy in practice
examples.explanation: In the first case, auto(ref) produces a std::string even though ref has type const std::string&: decay removes both the reference and the const. In the second case, [captured = auto(msg)] copies the string at the moment the lambda is created — subsequent changes to msg do not affect the captured value. In the third case, auto(arr) applies array-to-pointer decay and returns int*, which is exactly what happens when an array is passed by value to a template.

examples.title: Lambda specifiers without (): the C++23 change
examples.expectedOutput:
inside: 11
inside: 12
outside: 10
to_long: 3000000000
safe_neg(7): -7
pi: 3.14159
examples.explanation: Before C++23, [value] mutable { ... } would not compile: the compiler required [value]() mutable { ... }. The same applied to [] -> long long { ... } and [] constexpr { ... } — each needed an empty () before the specifier. C++23 dropped that requirement: parentheses are no longer needed when there are no parameters. Note that mutable only affects by-value captures: the lambda's own copy of value is incremented independently of the outer variable, which is why outside remains 10.

lessons.title: Preprocessor and Attributes: #elifdef, [[assume]], [[nodiscard]] on Constructors
lessons.motivation: Three small but practical C++23 additions eliminate specific friction points developers encounter regularly. The #elifdef directive removes the awkward #elif defined(...) in preprocessor conditional chains. The [[assume]] attribute lets you tell the compiler something it cannot prove on its own — for example, that an array length is a multiple of four — and unlock more aggressive optimisation. Extending [[nodiscard]] to constructors closes a dangerous gap in RAII types: the compiler can now warn when an RAII object is created as a temporary and immediately destroyed, never actually guarding the resource.
lessons.theory:
The #elifdef and #elifndef directives are shorthand for #elif defined(...) and #elif !defined(...) respectively. Before C++23 a preprocessor chain with multiple macro branches looked like this: #ifdef FOO / ... / #elif defined(BAR) / ... / #elif !defined(BAZ) / ... / #endif. The same chain can now be written more compactly: #ifdef FOO / ... / #elifdef BAR / ... / #elifndef BAZ / ... / #endif. The semantics are unchanged — this is a purely syntactic improvement that mirrors the already-existing #ifdef and #ifndef shorthand.

The [[assume(expr)]] attribute tells the compiler that, at this point in the program, expr is guaranteed to be true — without actually evaluating that expression at runtime. It is the standard, portable replacement for __builtin_unreachable() in GCC/Clang and __assume(expr) in MSVC. Typical uses: informing the compiler that an array size is positive and a multiple of a power of two (enabling SIMD loop unrolling without a remainder branch), that a pointer is aligned to a cache-line boundary, or that a value lies within a specific range. The critical caveat: if the assumption is false at runtime, the behaviour of the program is undefined — the compiler is entitled to generate any code relying on that premise. [[assume]] is an expert tool for code where invariants are known with certainty.

The [[nodiscard]] attribute has existed since C++17 for functions and types. C++23 permits it to be applied directly to a constructor. This is particularly valuable for RAII types whose entire purpose is their lifetime. The classic example is a guard object: auto guard = std::lock_guard(m) correctly holds the mutex locked for the duration of guard. But writing std::lock_guard(m) without naming the variable creates a temporary that is immediately destroyed — the mutex is locked and unlocked in the same expression, so the guard never actually protects anything. Before C++23 the compiler silently accepted this. With [[nodiscard]] on the constructor the compiler issues a warning, exactly as it does when the return value of a [[nodiscard]] function is discarded.

All three features share a common trait: they solve real, frequently encountered problems without introducing new semantics. #elifdef improves readability of preprocessor code; [[assume]] passes invariant knowledge to the optimiser; [[nodiscard]] on constructors catches the classic RAII mistake at compile time.

examples.title: #elifdef: selecting a platform branch in the preprocessor
examples.explanation: The preprocessor tests the macros in order. Because PLATFORM_LINUX is defined, the second branch is selected. #elifdef PLATFORM_LINUX is exactly equivalent to #elif defined(PLATFORM_LINUX) — the difference is purely in brevity. With the classic syntax you would write #elif defined(PLATFORM_LINUX), which becomes increasingly verbose as macro names grow longer.

examples.title: [[assume]]: hinting the compiler about data invariants
examples.explanation: The program output is identical with and without [[assume]] — the difference is in the generated machine code. Knowing n > 0, the compiler can eliminate the empty-loop check. Knowing n % 4 == 0, it can unroll the loop four iterations at a time with no leftover branch. If n actually violated these conditions the behaviour would be undefined — [[assume]] is not an assertion and does not check the condition; it only promises that the condition holds.

lessons.title: static_assert with String, std::is_implicit_lifetime, uz Literals
lessons.motivation: Three small but practical additions improve everyday C++ code. The restriction of static_assert to string literals disappears: the message can now be any constexpr expression — computed from template parameters, containing a type name, or depending on a condition. The std::is_implicit_lifetime<T> trait fills a gap in metaprogramming: you can now portably and standardly check whether it is safe to start an object's lifetime via memcpy without explicit placement new. Finally, the uz and z suffixes for integer literals eliminate a constant source of -Wsign-compare warnings: instead of the awkward cast (size_t)0, writing 0uz gives you a literal whose type automatically matches size_t on every platform.
lessons.theory:
Before C++23 — or more precisely before C++26, where P2741 eventually landed after further refinement — the second argument of static_assert was required to be a string literal: static_assert(cond, "message"). No variables, no constexpr strings, no computed values. The compiler rejected std::string_view or const char* even when the value was known at compile time. Starting with GCC 14 this restriction is lifted: the message can be any expression that, in a constant evaluation context, reduces to a type with data and size (such as std::string_view). In practice this allows building messages that depend on template parameters: if a template is instantiated with a type that is too large, the error can include computed text such as "type is large" or "type is small". GCC accepts this syntax with -std=c++23 as a forward-looking extension; in C++26 it becomes standard.

std::is_implicit_lifetime<T> (P2674, C++23) is a new trait in <type_traits>. A type is an implicit-lifetime type if its lifetime can begin implicitly — for example, when a memcpy is performed into suitable storage without explicit placement new. Such types include all scalar types (int, double, pointers), all aggregates (structs without user-provided constructors, without private non-static data members, etc.), and types with at least one trivial eligible constructor and a trivial destructor. A type with a virtual destructor or any other non-trivial destructor is not an implicit-lifetime type. The trait matters for safe low-level serialisation: before writing into raw memory via memcpy you can statically verify that the type supports this without undefined behaviour.

The uz and z integer literal suffixes (P0330, C++23) solve one of the most common causes of compiler warnings in code that works with container indices. Writing for (int i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) triggers -Wsign-compare: i is a signed int while v.size() is an unsigned size_t. The traditional fixes — (int)v.size() or size_t i = 0 — are either unsafe for large sizes or make reverse iteration awkward. The suffix uz produces a literal of type size_t: 0uz, 1uz, 42uz. The suffix z produces the signed counterpart — ptrdiff_t. On 64-bit systems both suffixes yield 8-byte types; on 32-bit systems they yield 4-byte types. This makes code portable: the type automatically matches the platform's address-space width. The expression std::views::iota(0uz, v.size()) builds an index range whose element type matches the container's size_type with no casts required.

examples.title: static_assert with a computed message and uz literals
examples.explanation: size_message<T>() is a consteval function evaluated at compile time. static_assert(sizeof(T) <= 8, size_message<T>()) uses its result as the error message — if the condition were violated, the compiler would display "type is large (>8 bytes)". Previously only string literals were accepted as the second argument. The uz suffix makes 0uz a literal of type size_t: the loop variable i has exactly the same type as v.size(), so no -Wsign-compare warning appears. The z suffix yields a signed type (ptrdiff_t), which is needed for the reverse loop: the condition i >= 0z compares signed values correctly and avoids the classic sign-comparison pitfall.

examples.title: std::is_implicit_lifetime and uz with views::iota
examples.expectedOutput:
int: true
double: true
Point (aggregate): true
Base (virtual dtor): false
Derived (virtual dtor): false
0: alpha
1: beta
2: gamma
examples.explanation: Scalar types (int, double) and trivial aggregates (Point) are implicit-lifetime types: their lifetime can begin through memcpy into suitable storage without explicit placement new. Base has a virtual destructor — non-trivial, so is_implicit_lifetime_v<Base> is false. Derived inherits that non-trivial destructor and is not implicit-lifetime either. In the lower half: std::views::iota(0uz, words.size()) produces a range of size_t values — the same type as words.size(). Without uz you would need a cast or risk a sign-mismatch warning.
